title: The Well of Echoes
body:
The road bends through gorse and thorn and ends at a low stone wall ringing an old well. The water within is dark and perfectly still. A child's iron cup lies on the rim, dry.

As you approach, you hear — faintly, as if from far down the shaft — a voice calling a name you almost recognize.

choices.label: Leave the well and ride on.

title: An Answer
body:
You lean over the stone and call. Your voice returns to you at once — and then again, late, changed. The second echo is a question, not a repetition: "Who asks?"

The hair lifts on your arms.

title: The Cup
body:
The cup is lighter than iron should be. You lower it on its chain — longer than the well looks deep — and draw it up brimming. The water tastes of cold stone and, faintly, of salt, though you are a hundred leagues from any sea.

When you set the cup down you see a single silver hair inside it, curled like a question mark.

choices.label: Keep the hair and ride on.
Return the hair to the well.

title: The Road Onward
body:
You remount and ride. Within a mile the gorse gives way to tilled land and the sound of the well is gone from your ears, though not, perhaps, from your memory.

(End of encounter.)

title: Truly Named
body:
The well is silent for a long breath. Then the water far below brightens — not with sun, for the sky is grey — and a face looks up at you that is almost your own but older, and sadder, and kind.

"Then I will remember you," the face says, and is gone.

You ride away knowing you have been seen, and that somewhere, something now keeps your name like a coin in a purse. Whether that is blessing or burden is not yet clear.

(End of encounter.)

title: Falsely Named
body:
The water stirs — the first movement you have seen in it. A laugh comes up, dry as old leaves. "A lie for a lie, then."

Something in you feels lighter, as though a small truth has been taken out and replaced with a stone of the same weight. You cannot yet say what you have lost. Perhaps nothing. Perhaps a thing you needed.

(End of encounter.)

title: The Hair
body:
You coil the silver hair in a scrap of cloth and tuck it into your belt pouch. As you ride on, the wind carries what might be a woman weeping, far behind you, and then nothing.

That night, beside your fire, the hair is still in the cloth. In the morning it is gone, and there is a fine line on the palm of your hand where no line was before.

(End of encounter.)

title: Returned
body:
You let the hair fall back into the cup, and the cup back into the dark. For a long moment nothing. Then, far below, a sound like a held breath being let out.

When you ride away you feel unaccountably lighter, as if some small debt — not yours, but one you were near enough to carry — has been paid on your behalf.

(End of encounter.)
